title: The Cutting Board Path
summary: Face grain to edge grain to end grain — the clearest skill ladder in woodworking.
description:
Three boards, and each one is genuinely harder than the last for a reason you can name.

Cutting boards are the best teaching project there is, because the same object gets progressively more demanding without ever getting more complicated. There is no new joinery here and no new hardware. What changes is your tolerance for error: a face-grain board forgives a sloppy glue line, and an end-grain board does not forgive anything.

By the end of this path you can flatten a panel, close a joint without clamping it into submission, and finish a surface that gets washed every day. Those three skills carry straight into furniture.

steps.reason: Face grain — the boards lie flat and the wide face is your surface. One glue-up, and the grooves teach you to control a router without much on the line. Start here even if it looks too easy: it is where you learn what a good glue joint LOOKS like.

Turn the strips on edge and everything gets stricter. Now the joint quality is visible on the working surface, and every edge has to be straight and square or the panel will not close without a gap. This is where most people learn that you fix the EDGES, not the clamps.

Two glue-ups, a crosscut in between, and end grain that drinks finish and dulls your tools. Every error from the last two boards is now multiplied and staring at you from the top face. Do not start here — you will hate it. Arrive here, and it is the most satisfying thing on this site.
